planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; stinking passionflower is a self-sowing annual to short-lived perennial that naturalizes readily in zone 9. The sticky, hairy leaves trap insects — a possible protocarnivorous adaptation.

directions: Direct sow in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; stinking passionflower is a weedy, delicate annual vine producing small white to pale lavender passion flowers enclosed in intricate, hair-fringed bracts that resemble lace or mist around the flower — giving it the poetic alternative name 'love-in-a-mist'. The red to orange ripe berries enclosed in the lacy bracts are edible and consumed by birds and children. The plant is a larval host for the gulf fritillary butterfly, adding to its conservation value. The leaves have a slightly unpleasant odor when crushed (hence 'foetida') and the glandular hairs on the bracts are sticky and may trap small insects. Self-sows prolifically in zone 9 to naturalize as a charming summer vine, flowering continuously through the season until frost.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Chinese Virginia creeper is one of the most shade-tolerant ornamental vines and is particularly effective as a self-clinging shade vine on north and east-facing walls.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Chinese Virginia creeper is a shade-adapted relative of the American Virginia creeper (P. quinquefolia), notable for its strikingly beautiful foliage — the dark green leaves are marked with silver-white veining along the main veins, creating a distinctive, elegant variegated effect. The velvety texture of the leaves enhances this decorative quality. In shade, the silver veining is most pronounced; in full sun, the plant is less attractive and the variegation less distinct. The vine clings to walls, fences, and tree trunks using adhesive tendrils. In fall, the leaves turn rich red-orange even in shade — unusual for a variegated plant. An outstanding shade vine for covering north-facing masonry walls where few other vines perform well.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Chinese climbing hydrangea is slow to establish (2–3 years before significant growth), but once established it becomes a long-lived, vigorous vine. It clings using aerial rootlets.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; Chinese climbing hydrangea is closely related to Japanese climbing hydrangea (Schizophragma hydrangeoides) but considered by many to be the more ornamentally impressive species, producing even larger, more spectacular flowerheads with broad, pure white sterile bracts that completely surround the central fertile flowers. The individual lacecap-like flower clusters can exceed 12 inches across. It clings to trees, walls, and trellises using aerial rootlets — no support structures needed. Like its relatives, it is slow to establish but becomes a spectacular, long-lived climber that requires no pruning to maintain. An outstanding shade vine for large masonry walls, mature trees, and shaded pergolas in zones 5–9.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring or bird-dispersed seeds in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; saw greenbrier is most useful as a wildlife plant in naturalized settings rather than as a cultivated garden plant — it spreads by extensive underground rhizomes and is very difficult to remove once established.

directions: Allow to naturalize in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in wildlife garden settings; saw greenbrier is a native evergreen vine of the southeastern US, producing stout, angular green stems armed with short, broad spines and thick, leathery, mottled-blotched dark green leaves. The small, shiny black berries in fall and winter are critically important food for mockingbirds, robins, cedar waxwings, bluebirds, and many other birds. The underground rhizomes form large, starchy masses that were roasted and eaten by southeastern Indigenous peoples; young shoots can be eaten as a cooked vegetable. Despite its ornamental berries and ecological importance, it is rarely cultivated — its stiff, sharp thorns and persistent rhizomes make it better suited to wildlife areas than manicured gardens.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or container stock in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, rich soil in partial shade to full sun; buckwheat vine grows naturally in moist to wet, alluvial, bottomland forests and swamp margins of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in naturalized settings along stream margins, pond edges, and moist woodland borders; buckwheat vine is a native vine of the southeastern US in the knotweed (Polygonaceae) family, producing distinctive, unusual fruit structures — each seed is enclosed in a winged, membranous structure resembling a miniature winged seed that aids wind dispersal, giving the plant an unusual ornamental quality in fruit. The white to cream flower clusters in summer attract bees and butterflies. In moist, shaded bottomland settings it can twine vigorously through shrubs and small trees, providing textural interest at the forest margin. An unusual and underappreciated native vine for naturalistic, moist woodland garden designs in zone 9a.
